file_name: Anand et al. - 2022 - An automated deep learning models for classification of skin disease using Dermoscopy images a comp.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses automated deep learning models, specifically pre-trained transfer learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including ResNet50, ResNet18, VGG16, and DenseNet121, to classify skin diseases (such as Melanocytic Nevi and Basal Cell Carcinoma) using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the evaluation of four pre-trained transfer learning CNN models (ResNet50, ResNet18, VGG16, DenseNet121) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin disease classification. It details specific methodology including data augmentation, hyperparameter tuning (epochs, batch size, optimizers), and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1-score) comparing model performance, rather than merely summarizing prior literature.

title: An automated deep learning models for classification of skin disease using Dermoscopy images: a comprehensive study
brief_summary: This study evaluates automated deep learning models for the classification of seven skin disease classes using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers assessed four pre-trained transfer learning Convolutional Neural Networks: ResNet50, ResNet18, VGG16, and DenseNet121. To address class imbalance, data augmentation was applied to the training set, and the models were optimized using different batch sizes (16 and 32) and optimizers (Adam and SGD). The evaluation metrics included accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score. The ResNet50 and ResNet18 models achieved the highest accuracy of 90% with a batch size of 32 and the SGD optimizer. The study concludes that these models can serve as effective second-opinion tools for the early diagnosis of skin diseases.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin disease classes (Actinic Keratoses, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, Vascular Lesions, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanocytic Nevi, Dermatofibroma, and Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The study performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset. The models (ResNet50, ResNet18, VGG16, DenseNet121) output predictions for one of seven specific diagnostic categories. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset containing 10,015 skin disease images with metadata. It was used as the primary source for training and testing the proposed deep learning models.

use.training: Used for training the pre-trained CNN models (ResNet50, ResNet18, VGG16, DenseNet121) after applying data augmentation techniques (flipping, rotation, brightness, zooming) to balance class distribution.
use.testing: Used for testing the models. The dataset was split into 90% training and 10% testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was compared using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

architecture_used.main_model: Pre-trained Transfer Learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16, and ResNet18

DenseNet121.description: Consists of four densely connected dense blocks

ResNet50.description: Includes 48 convolutional layers, 1 Maxpool layer, and 1 Average pool layer

ResNet18.description: Similar to ResNet50 but 18 layers deep

classification_or_output_head.modification: Last layer removed and replaced with a fully connected layer

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization: Input image pixel values (RGB format, range 0-255) were normalized to the range 0-1 by multiplying each pixel value with 1/255.

Data Augmentation: Applied to the training dataset to address class imbalance and increase sample size. Techniques included: horizontal flipping, vertical flipping, rotation (clockwise by 60, 120, and 180 degrees), brightness adjustment (levels 0.2, 0.3, 0.4), and zooming (factors 0.3, 0.4, 0.5).
Dataset Splitting: The dataset was divided into 90% for training and 10% for testing. Augmentation was applied only to the training set.

Images rotated in clockwise direction by angles of 60, 120, and 180 degrees.
Zoom factors applied with values of 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5.

Augmentation techniques were applied specifically to the training set to address class imbalance, increasing minority class samples (e.g., Dermatofibroma from 107 to 4455) to approximately 5000 images per class.

conclusions: The study concludes that automated deep learning models, specifically pre-trained transfer learning Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), can effectively classify seven skin diseases using dermoscopy images from the HAM10000 dataset.
Among the evaluated models (DenseNet121, ResNet50, VGG16, and ResNet18), ResNet50 achieved the best performance with an accuracy of 90% when using a batch size of 32 and the SGD optimizer.
ResNet18 also performed well, achieving an accuracy of 89% with the same optimal configuration (batch size 32, SGD optimizer).
DenseNet121 achieved a second-best accuracy of 89% and demonstrated high sensitivity (94%) specifically for Basal Cell Carcinoma.
Using a batch size of 32 and the SGD optimizer consistently yielded better accuracy and performance metrics compared to a batch size of 16 and the Adam optimizer across all tested models.
Data augmentation was necessary and effective in addressing class imbalance in the training dataset, increasing minority class samples (e.g., Dermatofibroma) to approximately 5000 images per class.
The proposed models can serve as effective second-opinion tools for dermatologists to support the early diagnosis of skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., 6705 Melanocytic Nevi vs. 115 Dermatofibroma) and potential label noise, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or datasets.

Data augmentation was applied only to the training set to balance classes, but no augmentation was applied to the testing set; this approach may lead to optimistic performance estimates if the test set distribution differs from the augmented training distribution.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals) for the comparison of model performances, making it difficult to assess whether the observed differences (e.g., 90% vs 89% accuracy) are statistically significant.

The dataset is heavily skewed towards Melanocytic Nevi (NV) class (6705 images), which may bias the models towards this class and reduce sensitivity for rare classes like Dermatofibroma (DF) and Vascular Lesions (VASC) despite augmentation.

The study claims the model can act as a 'second opinion tool' and for 'early diagnosis,' but provides no clinical validation, user study, or comparison with dermatologist performance, limiting the evidence for clinical relevance.

Misclassification analysis is limited to visual examples from ResNet50 without quantitative confusion matrix analysis for all classes, obscuring specific error patterns between visually similar classes (e.g., AKIEC vs. BCC).
The study uses a simple 90/10 train-test split without cross-validation, which may result in high variance in performance metrics depending on the specific split chosen.
The input image size is fixed at 224x224x3, which may discard important high-frequency dermoscopic details present in the original high-resolution images, potentially impacting diagnostic accuracy.
